other_side: This edition's lead is that AI's gains keep vanishing into hidden labour, and the fix is not a better tool but real organisational transformation — building human infrastructure so individual productivity becomes company performance. Zitron's argument cuts beneath that: the transformation story is itself ritual. He calls the tech industry "the largest cargo cult of all time" — Microsoft, Google, Amazon and Meta sinking what he says will soon top a trillion dollars into AI data centres "because they don't have any other ideas," then dressing up the spend as disruption. In his telling, narratives like the productivity dividend or the "SaaSpocalypse" are directionally-pleasing stories planted by executives and repeated by a credulous media to paper over a software industry already in decline, with growth efficiency halved and SaaS revenue down two percent year-on-year. Where Whittemore sees a hard transition worth doing the work to cross, Zitron sees a movement going through the motions of innovation while the returns never arrive.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether the money and effort going into enterprise AI is producing real organisational value or only its appearance. Both accept the spend is enormous and that, so far, most organisations report no significant improvement — this very edition concedes only thirteen percent say they are performing significantly better. The empirical question that will settle it is whether firms that genuinely restructure around AI show durable, audited gains in output and profit at true cost; the observable that resolves it is hard financial and productivity data once subsidies end, not survey sentiment or capex announcements. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore reads the bot-sitting era as solvable growing pains, Zitron reads the whole buildout as ritual whose gods are quietly dying. Whittemore is vindicated if transformative organisations convert AI into measured, lasting gains; Zitron if the trillion-dollar bet keeps failing to return and curdles into writedowns. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain with a paid newsletter — and the signal is that both agree individual AI use is not yet becoming organisational performance. Watch whether any large adopter publishes audited, priced-at-cost gains, or whether the capex starts showing up as impairments.
